coredata.dc:title: Bilateral efforts to improve SERS detection efficiency of exosomes by Au/Na7PMo11O39 Combined with Phospholipid Epitope Imprinting
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Qingnan
Cheng, Xianhui
Hu, Saizhen
Zhao, Menghan
Chen, Junjie
Mu, Ming
Yang, Yumei
Liu, Hao
Hu, Lianghai
Zhao, Bing
Song, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Detection of cancer-related exosomes in body fluids has become a revolutionary strategy for early cancer diagnosis and prognosis prediction. We have developed a two-step targeting detection method, termed PS-MIPs-NELISA SERS, for rapid and highly sensitive exosomes detection. In the first step, a phospholipid polar site imprinting strategy was employed using magnetic PS-MIPs (phospholipids-molecularly imprinted polymers) to selectively isolate and enrich all exosomes from urine samples. In the second step, a nanozyme-linked immunosorbent assay (NELISA) technique was utilized. We constructed Au/Na7PMo11O39 nanoparticles (NPs) with both surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) property and peroxidase catalytic activity, followed by the immobilization of CD9 antibodies on the surface of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs. The Au/Na7PMo11O39-CD9 antibody complexes were then used to recognize CD9 proteins on the surface of exosomes enriched by magnetic PS-MIPs. Lastly, the high sensitivity detection of exosomes was achieved indirectly via the SERS activity and peroxidase-like activity of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs. The quantity of exosomes in urine samples from pancreatic cancer patients obtained by the PS-MIPs-NELISA SERS technique showed a linear relationship with the SERS intensity in the range of 6.21 × 107–2.81 × 108 particles/mL, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 5.82 × 107 particles/mL. The SERS signal intensity of exosomes in urine samples from pancreatic cancer patients was higher than that of healthy volunteers. This bidirectional MIPs-NELISA-SERS approach enables noninvasive, highly sensitive, and rapid detection of cancer, facilitating the monitoring of disease progression during treatment and opening up a new avenue for rapid early cancer screening.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy
Nanozyme-linked immunosorbent assays
Molecular imprinted polymers
Exosomes
Polyoxometalates

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Exosome SERS detection platform based on PS-MIPs and NELISA combined technique
Coating of Anti-CD9 antibody on Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs
Recognition of magnetic PS-MIPs using Au/Na7PMo11O39-CD9 antibody complex
Peroxidase-like catalytic activity of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs
Exosome SERS detection platform based on PS-MIPs and NELISA combined technique

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by research grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22074051, 92161119 and 22374056).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of exosome SERS detection platform based on PS-MIPs and NELISA combined technique.

(a) Schematic diagram of synthesis process of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs, (b) TEM image of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs. (c) HRTEM images of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs. (d) SAED patterns of Au/Na7PMo11O39, XPS spectra of the Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs, (e) Au 4f, (f) Mo 3d and (g) O 1s.

(a) SERS spectra of different concentrations of 4-MBA molecules absorbed on Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs under 633 nm laser excitation. (b) Linear relationship between the SERS intensity at 1587 cm−1 of 4-MBA adsorbed on Au/Na7PMo11O39 and the logarithm of concentration. (c) SERS enhancement mechanism between Au/Na7PMo11O39 and 4-MBA. (d) Comparison of UV–visible spectra for TMB, TMB + H2O2, Au/Na7PMo11O39, and Au/Na7PMo11O39+TMB + H2O2. (e) Comparison of SERS spectra for TMB, TMB + H2O2, Au/Na7PMo11O39, and Au/Na7PMo11O39+TMB + H2O2. (f) Catalytic mechanism of Au/Na7PMo11O39-TMB.

(a) SERS spectra of different concentrations of exosomes obtained through ultracentrifugation and NELISA technology within the range of 6.21 × 107–2.81 × 108 particles/mL. (b) Linear fitting function between the SERS intensity at 1608 cm−1 and the corresponding concentrations. (c) SERS spectra of different volumes of exosomes within the range of 0.05–0.2 mL obtained through magnetic PS-MIPs and NELISA technology. (d) Linear fitting function between the SERS intensity at 1608 cm−1 and the corresponding volumes of pancreatic cancer patients (orange line), and linear fitting function between the SERS intensity at 1608 cm−1 and the corresponding number of exosomes of pancreatic cancer patients (blue line). Comparison of SERS intensity for exosomes in different patient samples: (e) histogram and (f) scatter plot.

head.ce:title: Bilateral efforts to improve SERS detection efficiency of exosomes by Au/Na7PMo11O39 Combined with Phospholipid Epitope Imprinting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Supramolecular Chemical Biology, State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, School of Life Sciences, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Supramolecular Chemical Biology, State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, School of Life Sciences, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, P. R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Q. Zhao and X.H. Cheng contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Detection of cancer-related exosomes in body fluids has become a revolutionary strategy for early cancer diagnosis and prognosis prediction. We have developed a two-step targeting detection method, termed PS-MIPs-NELISA SERS, for rapid and highly sensitive exosomes detection. In the first step, a phospholipid polar site imprinting strategy was employed using magnetic PS-MIPs (phospholipids-molecularly imprinted polymers) to selectively isolate and enrich all exosomes from urine samples. In the second step, a nanozyme-linked immunosorbent assay (NELISA) technique was utilized. We constructed Au/Na7PMo11O39 nanoparticles (NPs) with both surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) property and peroxidase catalytic activity, followed by the immobilization of CD9 antibodies on the surface of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs. The Au/Na7PMo11O39-CD9 antibody complexes were then used to recognize CD9 proteins on the surface of exosomes enriched by magnetic PS-MIPs. Lastly, the high sensitivity detection of exosomes was achieved indirectly via the SERS activity and peroxidase-like activity of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs. The quantity of exosomes in urine samples from pancreatic cancer patients obtained by the PS-MIPs-NELISA SERS technique showed a linear relationship with the SERS intensity in the range of 6.21 × 107–2.81 × 108 particles/mL, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 5.82 × 107 particles/mL. The SERS signal intensity of exosomes in urine samples from pancreatic cancer patients was higher than that of healthy volunteers. This bidirectional MIPs-NELISA-SERS approach enables noninvasive, highly sensitive, and rapid detection of cancer, facilitating the monitoring of disease progression during treatment and opening up a new avenue for rapid early cancer screening.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Liquid biopsy has the advantages of being minimally invasive, easy to collect samples, and dynamically analyze samples, and has become a revolutionary strategy for cancer diagnosis and prognosis prediction (Montironi et al., 2021; Pappas et al., 2022). Exosomes, with diameters of 40–160 nm, are lipid bilayer membrane vesicles actively released by most living cells and circulate stably in body fluids. They participate in many physiological and pathological processes, containing various information reflecting tumor progression, and are expected to become new biomarkers in liquid biopsy (Lima et al., 2021; Lin et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2021a,b). A major challenge in the application of exosomes in liquid biopsy is efficient and high purity separation and component analysis methods. Ultracentrifugation, as the gold standard method for exosome isolation, is the most commonly used method, but its application in clinical practice is largely limited by process time and requirements for large volumes of biological fluids (Pallares-Rusinol et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2021).

Phosphatidylserine (PS) is usually displayed on the surface of all exosomes. This is due to the separation of exosomes from the cytoplasm containing mitochondria and ATP generation mechanisms during germination, resulting in ATP depletion, thereby inhibiting ATP-dependent aminophospholipid flipping enzyme, and exposing PS to the outer surface of exosomes (Miao et al., 2014; Segawa et al., 2014; Nakai et al., 2016). Recently, we proposed a molecular imprinting polymers (MIPs) strategy targeting the polar head of phospholipids based on reverse phase microemulsion system (Zhou et al., 2023). The prepared PS-MIPs can capture exosomes from human body fluids by specifically binding to the hydrophilic head groups of PS exposed on the outer leaflet of the exosome membrane, and selectively isolate exosomes from complex biological fluids with magnetic separation efficiently.

Various proteins related to cancer and diseases, such as tetraspanins (CD9, CD63, and CD81) and heat shock proteins (HSP60, HSP70), are highly expressed on the membrane of cancer cell-secreted exosomes, and they are involved in the process of tumorigenesis and metastasis. CD9 has emerged as a critical indicator for prognostic assessment and metastatic potential in numerous types of cancers, including breast cancer and pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. (Koh et al., 2021; Lorico et al., 2021; Nigri et al., 2022; Jiang et al., 2021; Lima Moura et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2017). Analyzing the levels of cancer-related proteins on the surface of exosomes using enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) is crucial for cancer detection (Lu et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2022). However, this method is time consuming and requires large sample volumes. Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) is an ultra-sensitive and rapid vibrational spectroscopic detection method (Huang et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2022). SERS can be used for biomarker detection and cancer imaging (Dong et al., 2022a, 2022b; Zhang et al., 2023; Xiong et al., 2022). Combining nanozyme materials with SERS technology, which possess simulated enzyme properties, can greatly enhance the efficiency of ELISA detection analysis. For example, the use of Ag@GQDs and Au@CDs in nanozyme-linked immunosorbent assay (NELISA) combined with SERS analysis has improved the sensitivity of detecting biomolecules (Jin et al., 2022; Li et al., 2022), and the use of A-Au/LaNiO3-δ nanofibers has been applied in the detection of exosomes and cancer (Zhao et al., 2022). Polyoxometalates (POMs) have distinct molecular structures, good dispersibility, and excellent catalytic properties (Stamate et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2021a,b). Sub-group removal from POMs forms a vacancy, thereby altering the energy band, enhancing charge transfer, and consequently, enhancing its catalytic capabilities. It is expected that the combination of POMs and noble metals will achieve synergistic enhancement of SERS activity and nanozyme catalytic activity, which can be applied in NELISA-SERS detection.

We have established a PS-MIPs-NELISA SERS detection platform here for the detection of cancer-related exosomes. We have constructed Au/Na7PMo11O39 nanoparticles (NPs) with SERS activity and peroxidase-like activity and assembled CD9 antibodies. We use magnetic PS-MIPs to enrich exosomes in urine, and then use the Au/Na7PMo11O39-CD9 antibody complex to identify the enriched exosomes with cancer-related CD9 protein. The peroxidase-like activity and SERS activity of Au/Na7PMo11O39 are utilized to detect the quantity of exosomes with CD9 protein in urine. This novel exosome assay platform can rapidly and highly sensitively distinguish the treatment status of healthy individuals and cancer patients. This biosensor based on the MIPs-NELISA-SERS method opens up a new pathway for rapid early screening of cancer by detecting exosomes.

According to Zhou's method (Zhou et al., 2023), 1.724 mmol FeCl2·4H2O and 3.448 mmol FeCl3·6H2O were first dissolve in 100 g water. The solution was then added to a well-distributed toluene suspension 0.02 mol of N-hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) under nitrogen with vigorous stirring for 1 h. Subsequently, 1 mL ammonia was added to the mixture to form Fe3O4 nanocomposites. After adding 20 μmol PS as template molecules and stirring for 20 min, a mixture of 1140 μmol tetraethoxysilane and 60 μmol 3-ureidopropyltriethoxysilane (UPTES) was added and reacted for 6 h. The emulsion was broken by adding 20 mL acetone. Then, the template molecules and surfactants were removed from the prepared material by Soxhlet extraction with dialysis membrane (3.5 kDa MW cut off) for 24 h using 150 mL methanol with 1.5 mL HCl (37.2%) as the extraction solvent. Finally, the material was dried at 60 °C to obtain magnetic PS-MIPs.

According to the previous report (Vilanculo and Da Silva, 2020), 0.0365g of H3PMo12O40 was dissolved in 30 mL of water with strong acidity. NaHCO3 solution was added to adjust the pH to 4.8, obtaining a salt solution of Na7PMo11O39. The above mixture was stirred at 60 °C for 3 h. The synthesis of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs follows the method described in a previous report (Berbec et al., 2019). Specifically, 30 mL of Na7PMo11O39 and 2 mL of 0.01181g HAuCl4 were stirred in an ice bath for 30 min. Subsequently, 3 mL of 0.0017g NaBH4 was added dropwise using a syringe to the above mixture. The color of the solution gradually changed from colorless to reddish-brown. Finally, the composite was dialyzed for 48 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exosome SERS detection platform based on PS-MIPs and NELISA combined technique
ce:section.ce:para: We established a rapid and ultra-sensitive PS-MIPs-NELISA SERS detection platform using a two-step approximation detection method for cancer-related exosomes, as shown in Fig. 1 . The first step involves the utilizing magnetic PS-MIPs technology to isolate and enrich all exosomes from urine. This is based on a reverse microemulsion directional molecular imprinting technique, employing a phospholipid-polar site imprinting strategy with PS as the template molecule (Zhou et al., 2023). The constructed PS-MIPs can selectively recognize the membrane and selectively separate exosomes from complex biological fluids. The second step is to assemble the anti-CD9 antibody on the surface of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs, use the nanozyme-linked immunosorbent assay (NELISA) technology to recognize the magnetic PS-MIPs with the Au/Na7PMo11O39-CD9 antibody complex. Finally, the high sensitivity detection of exosomes was achieved indirectly using the SERS activity and peroxidase-like activity of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs.

Urinary samples of 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, and 200 μL were obtained from 6 pancreatic cancer patients and 6 healthy individuals. The samples were centrifuged at 5000 g for 10 min at 4 °C to remove larger interfering substances such as proteins. The resulting supernatant was collected and dispersed in 1 mL of PBS buffer at pH 7.4.5.0 mg of magnetic PS-MIPs were dissolved in the aforementioned solution and incubated on a rotary shaker for 1 h. The magnetic particles were then separated using a magnet, washed three times with PBS, and finally resuspended in 1 mL of PBS. Each experiment was conducted in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coating of Anti-CD9 antibody on Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Following the method by (Zhao et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2018), Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs were coupled with anti-CD9 antibody through electrostatic adsorption. The specific procedure is as follows: 5 μg of anti-CD9 antibody was added to 1 mL of Au/Na7PMo11O39 solution (the Au concentration calculated via ICP data was 7.5 × 10−5 mmol/mL) and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h to prepare antibody-bound Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs. Finally, the anti-CD9 antibody-functionalized Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs were obtained by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 10 min and dispersed in 1 mL of PBS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition of magnetic PS-MIPs using Au/Na7PMo11O39-CD9 antibody complex
ce:section.ce:para: 10 μL of the above solution was mixed with 200 μL of Au/Na7PMo11O39-CD9 antibody complex and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. The magnetic particles were then separated using a magnet and washed three times with PBS. Next, 20 μL of 3,3,5,5-tetramethybenzidine (TMB) and 20 μL of H2O2 were added to the solution, and after a 40-min reaction, the SERS intensity of the oxidized TMB (oxTMB) was measured. This allowed for indirect quantification of the exosome concentration.

The synthesis process of Au/Na7PMo11O39 is illustrated in Fig. 2 a. An HAuCl4 solution is added to a Na7PMo11O39 solution, and then a reducing agent, NaBH4, is slowly added dropwise in an ice bath to obtain Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs. Due to the negative charge on the surface of [PMo11O39]7-, it can act as a stabilizer and assembly agent, facilitating the assembly of Au NPs during the reduction process to form Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images presented in Figs. S1 and 2b illustrate the diameter of the Na7PMo11O39 NPs to be approximately 1–2 nm, the Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs exhibit a spherical shape, with a diameter of 25 ± 5 nm. Notably, Na7PMo11O39 is observed wrapping around the Au nanoparticles to form a shell structure. The high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 2c reveals lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.237 nm, which is consistent with the (111) crystal plane of Au. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern in Fig. 2d provides additional diffraction rings, corresponding to the face-centered cubic phase of Au, as confirmed by XRD results. Due to the low crystallinity of Na7PW11O39, only the (111), (200), (220), and (311) Au crystal planes appear (Fig. S2). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to investigate the elemental composition and binding energies of Au/Na7PMo11O39. The XPS spectra of Au/Na7PMo11O39 exhibit the presence of Au, P, Mo, and O elements (Fig. S3a) (Nadaf et al., 2021). Two distinct binding energies at 83.3 eV and 87.0 eV are observed in the Au 4f XPS spectrum, corresponding to Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2, respectively (Fig. 2e) (Wen et al., 2020), confirming the formation of Au/Na7PMo11O39.The P 2p peak is observed at a binding energy of 133.7 eV with only one component (Fig. S3b). The two binding energies of Mo 3d at 232.5 eV and 235.8 eV are assigned to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2, respectively (Fig. 2f) (Wang et al., 2009). Additionally, the O1s XPS spectrum of Au/Na7PMo11O39 shows peaks at 530.5, 531.7, and 532.2 eV, corresponding to lattice oxygen, oxygen vacancies, and chemisorbed oxygen, respectively (Fig. 2g). We were unable to detect obvious Raman peaks in the Raman spectra of both Na7PMo11O39 and Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs (Fig. S4). FTIR spectra of Au/Na7PMo11O39 (Fig. S5) show absorption peaks at 714, 841, and 1071 cm−1, which can be attributed to the vibrational modes v(Mo-Oc-Mo), V(Mo-Ob-Mo), and V(P-Oa) of PMo12 (Spojakina et al., 2005). The absorption bands at 1630 and 3427 cm−1 are assigned to the vibrational modes of COOH and NH2 groups (Ch'ng et al., 2011; Chandran et al., 2011). The two absorption peaks at 2920 and 2850 cm−1 arise from the stretching vibration of C–H bonds (Zhao et al., 2019).

4-mercaptobenzoic acid (4-MBA) was used as a probe molecule to evaluate the SERS activity of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs (Fig. 3 a). The surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption peak of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs was around 500–800 nm (Fig. 3d), so we chose a 633 nm excitation line to detect the SERS spectra of 4-MBA adsorbed on Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs. The peaks at 1074, 1184, 1377, and 1589 cm−1 in the SERS spectrum were attributed to the C–S stretching vibration mode, CH deformation mode, COO stretching vibration mode, and asymmetric C–H in-plane bending vibration of 4-MBA (Han et al., 2018; Pan et al., 2021). Under 633 nm excitation, the SERS of 4-MBA adsorbed at different concentrations was detected, and the peak intensity at 1587 cm−1 increased with increasing concentration. The linear range was 6 × 10−7 to 4 × 10−5 M, with an R2 of 0.9797, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 2.9 × 10−7 M (Fig. 3a and b). As we all know, the enhancement mechanisms of surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) can be divided into electromagnetic (EM) enhancement and charge transfer (CT) enhancement. For Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs, the main enhancement mechanism arises from the electromagnetic enhancement (EM) generated by the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect of the noble metal nanoparticles. The energy band and electronic structure of Na7PMo11O39 also influence the SERS activity of the substrate. Therefore, we further investigated the changes in the energy band structure of Na7PMo11O39 and Au/Na7PMo11O39 using Ultraviolet Photoelectron Spectroscopy (UPS) and UV–vis spectroscopy (Figs. S6 and S7). According to literature, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of 4-MBA are approximately −6.24 eV and −1.68 eV, respectively (Yang et al., 2017). The valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) energy levels of Na7PMo11O39 were determined to be approximately −6.34 eV and −3.14 eV, respectively; the CB and VB of Au/Na7PMo11O39 are −7.718 eV and −3.588 eV. Under 633 nm laser irradiation, electrons are difficult to transfer from the HOMO orbital of 4-MBA to the CB of Au/Na7PMo11O39, can only be transferred from the HOMO orbital of 4-MBA to the Fermi level of Au and then to the CB of Na7PMo11O39 (Fig. 3c and S8). These results indicate that the SERS activity of Au/Na7PMo11O39 is attributed to the synergistic effect of EM and CT processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-like catalytic activity of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic activity of Au/Na7PMo11O39 as a peroxidase mimic was further investigated using TMB as a substrate. UV–Vis spectra depicted in Fig. 3d and S9 demonstrate that the 650 nm oxTMB absorption peak formed from TMB catalyzed by Au + H2O2 and Na7PMo11O39+H2O2 displays considerably weaker oxTMB intensity when compared to that generated from Au/Na7PMo11O39+H2O2, indicating superior synergistic peroxidase-like catalytic efficiency of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs. Fig. S10 visually illustrates the time-dependent and linear relationship of the absorbance of oxTMB subjected to Au/Na7PMo11O39 catalytic processing, which was continuously monitored through UV–vis measurements at room temperature. SERS spectra can provide more vibrational information of the adsorbed molecules on the substrate. Therefore, further studies on the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of Au/Na7PMo11O39 were conducted using SERS spectra, as shown in Fig. 3e. The peaks at 1190, 1335, and 1608 cm−1 were attributed to the C–N stretching vibration, C–H bending vibration, and CN and CCC stretching vibrations of oxTMB, respectively (Pham et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2019). The SERS intensity at 1608 cm−1 of oxTMB increased with the reaction time (Fig. S11), reached a steady state after 30 min. To further elucidate the catalytic mechanism of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs for the oxidation of TMB, their normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) was studied. Based on Equations S4 and S5, the energy positions of the conduction band (ECB) and valence band (EVB) of Na7PMo11O39 NPs can be calculated (Fig. S12). Therefore, the catalytic mechanism is shown in Fig. 3f, where Au/Na7PMo11O39 catalyzes H2O2 into •OH radicals, and •OH radicals oxidize TMB into oxTMB molecules.

The quantity of oxTMB, as well as the SERS signal intensity, varies with different doses of Au/Na7PMo11O39 catalyst. We characterized Au/Na7PMo11O39 using inductively coupled plasma (ICP) to obtain the concentrations of each element in Au/Na7PMo11O39 and calibrated the catalytic activity of the nanozyme using the concentration of the Au element (Table S1). As demonstrated in Fig. S13, there is a positive correlation between the SERS intensity at 1608 cm−1 and the concentration of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs, showing a linear relationship with a linear equation of Y = 429.2X - 348.5. This analysis yielded a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.981, along with a detection limit of 1.37 × 10−7 mmol/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exosome SERS detection platform based on PS-MIPs and NELISA combined technique
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the extremely low content of exosomes in body fluids, we established a PS-MIPs-NELISA SERS rapid and ultra-sensitive detection platform using a two-step approximation detection method for cancer-related exosomes, as shown in Fig. 1. PS-MIPs can specifically identify PS on the cell membrane surface (Zhou et al., 2023). We isolated and enriched all exosomes from urine using magnetic PS-MIPs technology. Compared with the traditional gold standard for exosome isolation-ultracentrifugation, it is more efficient in capturing exosomes from high ionic strength body fluid samples. We also performed Western blot analysis of exosomes captured by ultracentrifugation and PS-MIPs affinity using CD9, TSG101 and CD81 antibodies. The results showed that PS-MIPs were more efficient than ultracentrifugation in capturing exosome proteins, as shown in Fig. S14. We assembled the CD9 antibody on the surface of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NP, as shown in Fig. S15 and Table S2. The Zeta potentials of Au/Na7PMo11O39 and Au/Na7PMo11O39-CD9 antibody were −33.5 and −28 mV, respectively, indicating the electrostatic binding of Au/Na7PMo11O39 with CD9 antibody. Subsequently, the Au/Na7PMo11O39-CD9 antibody complex was used to identify the CD9 protein on the cancer-related magnetic PS-MIPs enriched exosomes. This step further narrows down the range of samples to the quantity of exosomes associated with cancer. Finally, the high sensitivity detection of exosomes was achieved indirectly using the SERS activity and peroxidase-like activity. Since there is no obvious Raman peak in the MIPs, we evaluated the SERS of oxTMB with the MIPs-Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs system, Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs and Au colloids (Figs. S16 and S17). It was found that the MIPs-Au/Na7PMo11O39 system exhibited a significantly enhanced SERS intensity compared with the Au/Na7PMo11O39 and Au colloids system. This result can be attributed to the aggregation of Au/Na7PMo11O39 around the magnetic MIPs, resulting in a hotspot effect that amplifies the LSPR effect and thus increases its SERS intensity.

To establish the correlation between SERS and the quantity of exosomes, we used urine samples from healthy individuals and measured the concentration and size of exosomes particles using a Flow NanoAnalyzer instrument. Due to the low level of exosomes in urine, which could affect direct testing results, we isolated exosomes from 30 mL of urine from healthy individuals using ultracentrifugation, concentrating the exosome content. The obtained exosomes precipitate was dissolved in 15 mL of PBS, and the concentration and size of exosomes particles were measured, with a distribution curve plotted (see Fig. S18). The number of exosomes diluted in a 1:1 PBS solution was 3.25 × 108. Fig. S19 shows that there is an optimal linear relationship between the number of exosomes at different dilution ratios and the number of exosomes particles obtained from the instrument, with a linear equation of Y = 24.365X+4.99 and a linear range of 4.51 × 107–5.18 × 108 particles/mL, indicating that the number of exosomes particles within this range is proportional to the dilution concentration.

Furthermore, utilizing NELISA technology with the Au/Na7PMo11O39-CD9 antibody complex, we detected different quantities of exosomes obtained from ultracentrifugation. Fig. 4 a and b displays the SERS spectra of exosomes from human subjects. The SERS peak intensity increases with the increase in the quantity of exosomes from individuals, with a linear equation of Y = 264.9X-1363 and a linear range of 6.21 × 107 to 2.81 × 108 particles/mL and a limit of detection (LOD) of 5.82 × 107 particles/mL. Compared to previous studies, this PS-MIPs and NELISA combined technique for exosomes SERS detection platform shows higher sensitivity and lower LOD (see Table S3).

Pancreatic cancer is one of the most common cancers in humans, and early diagnosis of cancer is crucial (Rahal and Musher, 2017). We mixed exosomes from urine samples of different pancreatic cancer patients and took aliquots of the mixture with volumes of 0.05, 0.075, 0.1, 0.125, 0.15, and 0.2 mL. We used magnetic PS-MIPs to isolate and enrich all exosomes from these urine mixtures. Utilizing NELISA technology with the Au/Na7PMo11O39-CD9 antibody complex, we identified the CD9 protein on the exosomes separated and enriched by magnetic PS-MIPs, obtaining SERS spectra of exosomes volumes from different pancreatic cancer patient urine samples (Fig. 4c and Table S4). There exists a linear relationship between different exosomes volumes and SERS intensity, with an equation of Y = 30982.9X-142.87 (orange line in Fig. 4d). According to the linear equations, the exosomes numbers obtained by ultracentrifugation from urine of healthy individuals and their SERS intensity yield a linear equation of Y = 2649X-136 (blue line in Fig. 4d).

To validate the clinical applicability of this method, we employed the MIPs-NELISA-SERS technique to detect exosomes from five cancer patients and five healthy individuals. Their histograms and scatter plots are depicted in Fig. 4e and f. It can be observed that the SERS signal intensity of exosomes in the urine of pancreatic cancer patients is higher than that of healthy volunteers. This bidirectional MIPs-NELISA-SERS method enables non-invasive, highly sensitive, and rapid detection of pancreatic cancer, aiding in monitoring disease progression during treatment. This represents a significant advancement in utilizing exosomes for cancer detection.

We employed a one-step NaBH4 reduction method to construct Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs, which exhibit SERS activity and peroxidase-like catalytic activity. Additionally, we utilized a PS as the template molecule to develop magnetic PS-MIPs based on the phospholipid polar site imprinting strategy. Due to the extremely low content of exosomes in bodily fluids, we established a PS-MIPs-NELISA-SERS rapid and ultra-sensitive exosomes detection platform using a two-step sequential detection approach. The first step involved utilizing magnetic PS-MIPs technology to isolate and enrich all exosomes from urine. The second step incorporated NELISA technology, where CD9 antibodies were assembled on the surface of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs, followed by the use of the Au/Na7PMo11O39-CD9 antibody complex to identify the CD9 protein on the magnetically enriched exosomes associated with cancer. This step further narrowed down the range of samples to exosomes relevant to cancer. Finally, high sensitivity detection of exosomes was achieved indirectly through the SERS activity and peroxidase-like activity of Au/Na7PMo11O39 NPs. The pancreatic cancer patient urine exosome quantity obtained using the PS-MIPs-NELISA SERS technique exhibited a linear relationship with SERS intensity, with an equation of Y = 264.9X-1363, a linear range of 6.21 × 107–2.81 × 108 particles/mL, and a limit of detection (LOD) of 5.82 × 107 particles/mL. We also applied this technique to detect exosomes from five cancer patients and five healthy individuals, revealing that the SERS signal intensity of exosomes in the urine of pancreatic cancer patients was higher than that of healthy volunteers. This bidirectional MIPs-NELISA-SERS method enables non-invasive, highly sensitive, and rapid cancer detection, aiding in monitoring disease progression during treatment and paving the way for rapid early screening of cancer.
The collection of human urinary samples was approved by the ethics committee of the First Hospital of Jilin University (No. 2021–162).

Qingnan Zhao: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Software, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Xianhui Cheng: Formal analysis, Methodology. Saizhen Hu: Formal analysis. Menghan Zhao: Data curation. Junjie Chen: Formal analysis. Ming Mu: Methodology. Yumei Yang: Software. Hao Liu: Software. Lianghai Hu: Conceptualization, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Bing Zhao: Writing – review & editing. Wei Song: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by research grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22074051, 92161119 and 22374056).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is/are the supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
